learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a good working understanding of the broad field of intellectual property, which will provide the foundations for more specialised study.
Clearly understand the international framework within which domestic intellectual property law functions.
Understand and articulate the conceptual underpinnings and tensions within many areas of intellectual property.
Articulate the key legislative and caselaw features of Australian intellectual property law, in particular copyright, marks, patents, designs and confidential information.
Effectively use both hardcopy and electronic sources of law to research often complex legal problems.
Communicate effectively, both in written and oral form, whether in a work or academic environment, such that the knowledge and skills acquired in the course can be effectively used.

assessments.task_description:
Online quizzes will be given each week during the seminar with the exception of the first meeting. The highest 5 scores will count towards your mark in this assessment. These 'pop' quizzes will be announced at random times during the seminar meeting.

Each quiz will consists of 4 true/false or multiple-choice questions and can be accessed through the course Blackboard site. You will be given a limited time to answer the questions, which will be based on the assigned readings and lectures for that week.

Answers will be discussed during the seminar immediately after the quiz has ended. Your lowest two quiz scores will be dropped in calculating your overall score for this piece of assessment. Any missed quizzes due to absences for any reason will be counted as zero.

The purpose of the quizzes will be to assess the student's attendance and engagement during course seminars, as well as their understanding of material. They will also help the instructor understand student progress and address any gaps that become apparent.

NB: There will be a practice quiz in the first meeting that will not count towards assessment marks, but will serve as practice for students to navigate the online process.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Quizzes will be available through the course Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
At three specific points of time during the term, students will be provided with an essay-based problem that draws upon materials covered during the seminars. Responses to the problem will require the application of current law to a hypothetical scenario. Essay style responses must be answered in no more than 600 words for each problem. Each problem will be uploaded onto the Blackboard site on Thursdays of Weeks 6, 8 and 9, and responses will be due on the following Fridays at 2.00 pm.

A word count must appear on the front page of your work. Words in excess of the word limit will not be read or taken into account in marking the assessment.

The word limit does not include footnotes or bibliography. However, substantive material should appear in the body of the essay and footnotes should be used primarily for citational purposes.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Essays must be submitted electronically via the submission link on the course Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students will be provided with essay prompts, short answers, and multiple choice questions covering topics from throughout the course during this final exam. Students should expect an exam with limited or no choice, covering the whole semester's work.

The exam will be designed to be undertaken in 90 minutes with an expected preparation time of 10 minutes.

The exam will be delivered using Inspera software, however, please note that students will be expected to attend campus for an invigilated exam.

Any written or printed material is permitted in the exam and this material may be annotated.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 2 To Week 13(03 Aug - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Seminars

The programme of seminars is set out in the Course Learning Guide. This is available from the course Blackboard site.

Topics of seminars for Part 1 of the course are:

  1. Introduction to Intellectual Property: Theoretical and historical frameworks & domestic and international sources of law (Week 2)
  2. Copyright I
  3. Copyright II
  4. Designs
  5. Patents I
  6. Patents II
  7. Trademarks
  8. Other related rights

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L06
